([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 小池 百合子, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Koike Yuriko*; born 15 July 1952) is a Japanese politician who has served as the [Governor of Tokyo](/wiki/Governor_of_Tokyo "Governor of Tokyo") since 2016. Previously, she also served as a member of the [House of Councillors](/wiki/House_of_Councillors_(Japan) "House of Councillors (Japan)") from 1992 to 1993, a member of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_(Japan) "House of Representatives (Japan)") from 1993 to 2016, [Minister of the Environment](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Environment_(Japan) "Minister of the Environment (Japan)") under [Junichiro Koizumi](/wiki/Junichiro_Koizumi "Junichiro Koizumi") from 2003 to 2006, and [Minister of Defense](/wiki/Minister_of_Defense_(Japan) "Minister of Defense (Japan)") under [Shinzo Abe](/wiki/Shinzo_Abe "Shinzo Abe") between July and August 2007.

**Yuriko Koike** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 小池 百合子, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Koike Yuriko*; born 15 July 1952) is a Japanese politician who has served as the [Governor of Tokyo](/wiki/Governor_of_Tokyo "Governor of Tokyo") since 2016. Previously, she also served as a member of the [House of Councillors](/wiki/House_of_Councillors_(Japan) "House of Councillors (Japan)") from 1992 to 1993, a member of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_(Japan) "House of Representatives (Japan)") from 1993 to 2016, [Minister of the Environment](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Environment_(Japan) "Minister of the Environment (Japan)") under [Junichiro Koizumi](/wiki/Junichiro_Koizumi "Junichiro Koizumi") from 2003 to 2006, and [Minister of Defense](/wiki/Minister_of_Defense_(Japan) "Minister of Defense (Japan)") under [Shinzo Abe](/wiki/Shinzo_Abe "Shinzo Abe") between July and August 2007.

Born and raised in [Ashiya](/wiki/Ashiya,_Hy%C5%8Dgo "Ashiya, Hyōgo"), a wealthy, small, and popular city near [Kobe](/wiki/Kobe "Kobe") in [Hyōgo Prefecture](/wiki/Hy%C5%8Dgo_Prefecture "Hyōgo Prefecture"), Koike graduated from [Cairo University](/wiki/Cairo_University "Cairo University") in [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") in 1976, and served as a member of the [House of Representatives of Japan](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_(Japan) "House of Representatives (Japan)") from 1993 until 2016, when she resigned to run for Governor of Tokyo. Previously, she also served as the [Minister of the Environment](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Environment_(Japan) "Minister of the Environment (Japan)") under [Junichiro Koizumi](/wiki/Junichiro_Koizumi "Junichiro Koizumi")'s cabinet from 2003 to 2006 and briefly as [Minister of Defense](/wiki/Minister_of_Defense_(Japan) "Minister of Defense (Japan)") under the [first cabinet](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%AC%AC1%E6%AC%A1%E5%AE%89%E5%80%8D%E5%86%85%E9%96%A3 "ja:第1次安倍内閣") of [Shinzo Abe](/wiki/Shinzo_Abe "Shinzo Abe") in between July and August 2007.[[1]](#cite_note-japannewsreview1756-1)

Koike was [elected](/wiki/2016_Tokyo_gubernatorial_election "2016 Tokyo gubernatorial election") Governor of Tokyo in 2016, becoming the metropolis' first female governor.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Koike was re-elected in [2020](/wiki/2020_Tokyo_gubernatorial_election "2020 Tokyo gubernatorial election") and [2024](/wiki/2024_Tokyo_gubernatorial_election "2024 Tokyo gubernatorial election"), winning 59.7% and 42.8% of the popular vote respectively.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Considered one of the most high-profile and well-known Japanese politicians,[[5]](#cite_note-5) Koike has been frequently mentioned as holding Prime Ministerial ambitions.[[6]](#cite_note-6) She ran in the [2008 Liberal Democratic Party leadership election](/wiki/2008_Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan)_leadership_election "2008 Liberal Democratic Party (Japan) leadership election"), becoming the first woman to run for the leadership of a major Japanese political party, however she came in third place losing to [Tarō Asō](/wiki/Tar%C5%8D_As%C5%8D "Tarō Asō"). In 2017, Koike left the [LDP](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)") amid much media attention and launched two parties: the national party, [Kibō no Tō](/wiki/Kib%C5%8D_no_T%C5%8D "Kibō no Tō") (希望の党/Party of Hope) and the regional party [Tomin First no Kai](/wiki/Tomin_First_no_Kai "Tomin First no Kai") (都民ファーストの会/Tokyoites First Party). Kibō no Tō contested the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_Japanese_general_election "2017 Japanese general election") with Koike as a leader, however the party underperformed expectations and mostly disappeared after merging with the [Democratic Party for the People](/wiki/Democratic_Party_for_the_People "Democratic Party for the People") in 2018. The same year, Koike stepped down as leader of Tomin First and officially became [independent](/wiki/Political_independent "Political independent"). However, Koike continues to endorse and campaign for Tomin First candidates in Tokyo and the party still makes frequent use of her image and policies.

Koike has come under some scrutiny from [Japanese liberals](/wiki/Liberalism_in_Japan "Liberalism in Japan") and [Koreans](/wiki/Koreans "Koreans") in both Japan and Korea for her refusal to acknowledge the occurrence of 1923 [Kantō Massacre](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_Massacre "Kantō Massacre"), which mainly targeted ethnic Koreans, as well as her association with groups that are often labeled anti-Korean.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yuriko_Koike&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Koike was born on 15 July 1952 in [Ashiya](/wiki/Ashiya,_Hy%C5%8Dgo "Ashiya, Hyōgo") near [Kobe](/wiki/Kobe "Kobe") in [Hyōgo](/wiki/Hy%C5%8Dgo_Prefecture "Hyōgo Prefecture"), Koike went to Kōnan Girls' Junior and Senior High School for her secondary education. Her father, Yūjirō Koike, was a foreign trade merchant who handled oil products. He was also involved in politics, supporting [Shintarō Ishihara](/wiki/Shintar%C5%8D_Ishihara "Shintarō Ishihara") and the [Tatenokai](/wiki/Tatenokai "Tatenokai") in the 1960s, and ran unsuccessfully for [Japanese general election](/wiki/1969_Japanese_general_election "1969 Japanese general election") in 1969.[[8]](#cite_note-asahi20080824-8) Yūjirō emphasised to Yuriko that it was essential for Japan to strengthen relations with [Arab countries](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") to ensure a stable petroleum supply to prevent Japan being thrust into an [oil war](/wiki/Oil_war "Oil war") again in the future. After dropping out of [Kwansei Gakuin University](/wiki/Kwansei_Gakuin_University "Kwansei Gakuin University")'s School of Sociology in September 1971, she went on to study Arabic at the [American University in Cairo](/wiki/American_University_in_Cairo "American University in Cairo") and allegedly[[9]](#cite_note-9) received a [Bachelor of Arts](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") in [Sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") as the top student from [Cairo University](/wiki/Cairo_University "Cairo University") in October 1976.[[8]](#cite_note-asahi20080824-8)[[10]](#cite_note-josei-10) When she was 21, she married a fellow Japanese student but divorced soon after.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) She began to work as an interpreter of [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") and later became a journalist, interviewing [Muammar Gaddafi](/wiki/Muammar_Gaddafi "Muammar Gaddafi") and [Yasser Arafat](/wiki/Yasser_Arafat "Yasser Arafat") in 1978,[[13]](#cite_note-13) and becoming a news anchor in 1979. She received the Female Broadcaster of Japan award in 1990.

### Graduation from Cairo University

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yuriko_Koike&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Graduation from Cairo University")]

Koike has been alleged several times of [falsifying her credentials](/wiki/Job_fraud "Job fraud"), particularly regarding her graduation from [Cairo University](/wiki/Cairo_University "Cairo University") in [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"). In June 2020, Cairo University released a statement that "Cairo University certifies that Yuriko Koike... graduated from the Department of Sociology, Faculty of Arts, Cairo University in October 1976," denying the allegations.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Meanwhile, Egyptian allegations that she had committed job fraud first surfaced during 1992,[[15]](#cite_note-:2-15) they were more widely reported during the [Tokyo's gubernatorial election](/wiki/2016_Tokyo_gubernatorial_election "2016 Tokyo gubernatorial election") in 2016, and first reported by Japanese news program *[Tokudane!](/wiki/Tokudane! "Tokudane!")*. Koike sent her graduation certificate to the program in response to allegations.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) In January 2018, a woman who claimed to have been Koike's roommate at Cairo University wrote to the Japanese monthly magazine *[Bungei Shunjū](/wiki/Bungei_Shunj%C5%AB_(magazine) "Bungei Shunjū (magazine)")* after reading an article on Koike.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-:3-19)[[15]](#cite_note-:2-15) In 2024, the magazine published an article on their website, including a video where Toshiro Kojima, a lawyer and former aide of Koike, said that he helped to falsify Koike's academic credentials including drafting a fake graduation document.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Koike has denied the allegations.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Political career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yuriko_Koike&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Political career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Koike_-_Rice_usa-600.jpg/250px-Koike_-_Rice_usa-600.jpg)](/wiki/File:Koike_-_Rice_usa-600.jpg)

Koike, dubbed "Japan's Condi Rice",[[22]](#cite_note-japantimes-22) shakes hands with [Condoleezza Rice](/wiki/Condoleezza_Rice "Condoleezza Rice") in August 2007.

After her graduation in Egypt, Koike was elected to the [House of Councillors](/wiki/House_of_Councillors "House of Councillors") in [1992](/wiki/1992_Japanese_House_of_Councillors_election "1992 Japanese House of Councillors election") as a member of the [Japan New Party](/wiki/Japan_New_Party "Japan New Party"). She was then elected to the House of Representatives in [1993](/wiki/1993_Japanese_general_election "1993 Japanese general election"), representing the [Hyogo 2nd district](/wiki/Hyogo_2nd_district "Hyogo 2nd district"). In [1996](/wiki/1996_Japanese_general_election "1996 Japanese general election"), she was re-elected to the House of Representatives, this time representing the [Hyogo 6th district](/wiki/Hyogo_6th_district "Hyogo 6th district") for the [New Frontier Party](/wiki/New_Frontier_Party_(Japan) "New Frontier Party (Japan)"). She held this seat in the [2000 election](/wiki/2000_Japanese_general_election "2000 Japanese general election") as a candidate of the [New Conservative Party](/wiki/New_Conservative_Party_(Japan) "New Conservative Party (Japan)"). She joined the [Liberal Democratic Party](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)") in 2002.[[23]](#cite_note-23) She also has been a regular contributor to *[Project Syndicate](/wiki/Project_Syndicate "Project Syndicate")* since 2010.

### Cabinet service (2003–2007)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yuriko_Koike&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cabinet service (2003–2007)")]

She served as the [Minister of the Environment](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Environment_(Japan) "Ministry of the Environment (Japan)") and Minister of State for [Okinawa](/wiki/Okinawa_Prefecture "Okinawa Prefecture") and Northern Territories Affairs in the Cabinet of Prime Minister [Jun'ichirō Koizumi](/wiki/Jun%27ichir%C5%8D_Koizumi "Jun'ichirō Koizumi"). Along with [Satsuki Katayama](/wiki/Satsuki_Katayama "Satsuki Katayama") and [Makiko Fujino](/wiki/Makiko_Fujino "Makiko Fujino"), Koike became known as one of Koizumi's "assassins" in the [2005 Lower House election](/wiki/2005_Japanese_general_election "2005 Japanese general election"), running in Tokyo against an LDP hardliner candidate who opposed Koizumi's policies.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

She was appointed the first female Minister of Defense in June 2007 during the first term of Prime Minister [Shinzo Abe](/wiki/Shinzo_Abe "Shinzo Abe"), but announced in August 2007 that she intended to resign from the post, citing the Aegis classified information leak scandal as a reason.[[1]](#cite_note-japannewsreview1756-1) Koike later hinted that the much-publicized fight she had had with [Chief Cabinet Secretary](/wiki/Chief_Cabinet_Secretary "Chief Cabinet Secretary") [Yasuhisa Shiozaki](/wiki/Yasuhisa_Shiozaki "Yasuhisa Shiozaki") over a vice-minister replacement was the real reason, as the opposition would use that to oppose a bill on Japan's terrorism laws.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### 2008 LDP leadership election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yuriko_Koike&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2008 LDP leadership election")]

On 8 September 2008, she launched her bid to become [president of the LDP](/wiki/President_of_the_Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "President of the Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)") and became the [first woman](/wiki/List_of_the_first_female_holders_of_political_offices_in_Asia "List of the first female holders of political offices in Asia") ever to seek the [premiership](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Japan "Prime Minister of Japan") in Japan's history: "I have received the enthusiastic support of my colleagues. In order to break through the deadlock facing Japanese society, I believe the country might as well have a female candidate. [Hillary](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") used the word 'glass ceiling' ... but in Japan, it isn't glass, it's an iron plate. I'm not [Mrs. Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher"), but what is needed is a strategy that advances a cause with conviction, clear policies and sympathy with the people."[[26]](#cite_note-26) In [the leadership election](/wiki/2008_Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan)_leadership_election "2008 Liberal Democratic Party (Japan) leadership election"), held on 22 September, [Tarō Asō](/wiki/Tar%C5%8D_As%C5%8D "Tarō Asō") won with 351 of the 527 votes; Koike placed third with 46 votes.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Governor of Tokyo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yuriko_Koike&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Governor of Tokyo")]

Following the resignation of Tokyo Governor [Naoki Inose](/wiki/Naoki_Inose "Naoki Inose") in December 2013, Koike was widely rumored to be a potential candidate for the [gubernatorial election](/wiki/2014_Tokyo_gubernatorial_election "2014 Tokyo gubernatorial election") expected to be held in February 2014, along with [Hideo Higashikokubaru](/wiki/Hideo_Higashikokubaru "Hideo Higashikokubaru"), [Hakubun Shimomura](/wiki/Hakubun_Shimomura "Hakubun Shimomura"), [Seiko Hashimoto](/wiki/Seiko_Hashimoto "Seiko Hashimoto") and [Yōichi Masuzoe](/wiki/Y%C5%8Dichi_Masuzoe "Yōichi Masuzoe").[[28]](#cite_note-28) She ultimately did not run, and Masuzoe won.

Governor Koike speaking in 2017

After Masuzoe announced his resignation in June 2016, Koike announced her intention to run in the [election for his successor](/wiki/2016_Tokyo_gubernatorial_election "2016 Tokyo gubernatorial election"). Koike stated that she would run "as an LDP lawmaker" but did not obtain the approval of the Tokyo LDP chapter before announcing her candidacy.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The LDP officially endorsed [Hiroya Masuda](/wiki/Hiroya_Masuda "Hiroya Masuda"), and its Tokyo chapter issued a notice that any members supporting Koike would be punished. Nonetheless, several prominent LDP politicians continued to back Koike, while senior leaders such as Shinzo Abe refrained from making speeches in support of either candidate.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Koike was elected [Governor of Tokyo](/wiki/Governor_of_Tokyo "Governor of Tokyo") on 31 July 2016, becoming the first woman in the post.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

On 21 August 2016, at the [2016 Summer Olympics closing ceremony](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics_closing_ceremony "2016 Summer Olympics closing ceremony"), Koike received the [Olympic Flag](/wiki/Olympic_Flag "Olympic Flag"), via [Thomas Bach](/wiki/Thomas_Bach "Thomas Bach"), from the mayor of Rio de Janeiro, [Eduardo Paes](/wiki/Eduardo_Paes "Eduardo Paes").

On 31 May 2017, in advance of the upcoming local elections, Koike resigned from the [Liberal Democratic Party](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)") and officially became the leader of [Tomin First no Kai](/wiki/Tomin_First_no_Kai "Tomin First no Kai") (Tokyoites First). Koike founded the group in 2016 in preparation for the elections and formed an alliance with [Komeito](/wiki/Komeito "Komeito") in an effort to secure a governing majority in Tokyo's parliament.[[32]](#cite_note-32) On 3 July 2017, the alliance took a majority in the prefectural election, pushing out the Liberal Democratic Party with a combined 79 seats of the 127-seat assembly.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Koike joined [Shinzo Abe](/wiki/Shinzo_Abe "Shinzo Abe")'s [cabinet](/wiki/Fourth_Abe_Cabinet "Fourth Abe Cabinet"), where the country led the government response to [COVID-19 pandemic in Japan](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Japan "COVID-19 pandemic in Japan"),[[34]](#cite_note-34) as well as the postponement of [2020 Tokyo Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") to 2021.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] She served as governor during the successful completion of the Olympics and Paralympics in 2021. She passed the Olympic flag to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") mayor [Anne Hidalgo](/wiki/Anne_Hidalgo "Anne Hidalgo") during the closing ceremony, marking the conclusion of the games. It was largely held [behind closed doors](/wiki/Behind_closed_doors_(sport) "Behind closed doors (sport)") with no public spectators permitted due to the declaration of a state of emergency in the [Greater Tokyo Area](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area") in response to the pandemic, the only Olympic Games to be held without official spectators.[[a]](#cite_note-36) The Games were the most expensive ever, with total spending of over $20 billion.[[36]](#cite_note-37) There have been 788 cases detected and reported by the Tokyo 2020 Organising Committee between 1 July and 8 September 2021, with 66 additional cases being detected among Games personnel before that date after the Committee started recording them at an unknown date. The cases have sparked concerns prior to the games. The bubble surrounding the Olympic Village has been described as having been broken after the first case occurred there in mid-July.

On 14 August 2022, Governor of Jakarta [Anies Baswedan](/wiki/Anies_Baswedan "Anies Baswedan") paid a working visit to Japan to meet with his counterpart from Tokyo, Yuriko Koike, to discuss the potential for cooperation in several fields, including for environment-friendly public transportation. Baswedan posted a picture of the meeting with Koike at the Tokyo City Hall accompanied by several delegates on his personal Instagram account. However, the governor did not furnish further details on the potential for cooperation between the two cities and the length of his stay in Japan. However, Baswedan did invite Koike to attend the Urban 20 forum as a [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20") side event in Jakarta at the end of August 2022. He later noted that [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") and [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") were sister cities, with longstanding relations. Hence, the meeting offered momentum to demonstrate commitment to intensifying relations between the two metropolitan cities.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

Koike announced on 29 August 2022 that Tokyo would begin implementation of the world's fastest mobile internet network. Leading the charge is Manabu Miyasaka, the newly appointed counselor to the governor on digital transformation of Tokyo and former chairman of Yahoo! Japan Corporation.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

Koike ran her platform based on seven zeros, which were basically socio-economic problems faced by residents of Tokyo. Out of these goals, she was able to reduce the number of children on waitlists to gain admission to daycares and cutting down the number of euthanized dogs and cats. However, critics say other goals like tackling the overwork culture, reducing crowding on rush hour trains, and getting rid of above-ground electricity poles have not yet been achieved.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

Koike and her preferred candidate, [Hirotada Ototake](/wiki/Hirotada_Ototake "Hirotada Ototake"), placed fifth in the [Tokyo 15th district](/wiki/Tokyo_15th_district "Tokyo 15th district") by-election of lower house. This has caused discussion to increase on if Koike is losing much of her reputation as an intensely popular governor. This also came ahead of the next [Tokyo gubernatorial election](/wiki/2024_Tokyo_gubernatorial_election "2024 Tokyo gubernatorial election") held in July 2024. The crushing loss Ototake faced was reported as potentially putting a damper on continuous rumors regarding her wish to return to national politics following her failure in the [2017 Japanese general election](/wiki/2017_Japanese_general_election "2017 Japanese general election").[[40]](#cite_note-41) However, exit polls projected after the 2024 Tokyo election that Koike would win a third term as governor.[[41]](#cite_note-ap070724-42)

On August 6, 2024, Koike fractured her left knee while making a ceremonial first pitch at a [Central League](/wiki/Central_League "Central League") baseball match at the [Meiji Jingu Stadium](/wiki/Meiji_Jingu_Stadium "Meiji Jingu Stadium"), forcing her to work remotely.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Koike supports [economic liberalism](/wiki/Economic_liberalism "Economic liberalism"), promotes administrative and budgetary reform, and insists on further advancement of the status of women in the working world. In promising the pursuit of women-friendly policies, she has stated, "I believe that pushing policies for women will be good for Tokyo and bring happiness to the capital." Her stated basic principles and stance regarding political reform are encompassed by "The 5 Cs: Check, Challenge, Change, Creative and Communication".[[43]](#cite_note-44) In terms of the economy, she has pushed for aggressive privatization of Japanese assets to diminish the government's debt burden. A strong turn towards IT development, natural sciences, sustainable infrastructure, and efficiency-based administrative reforms for public services were also on the docket.[[44]](#cite_note-45) She is also one of the main figures in Japan's [right-wing populist](/wiki/Right-wing_populism "Right-wing populism") camp.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-FarRight-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48) She is also sometimes referred to as "ultraconservative".[[48]](#cite_note-IPS-49)

### Environmentalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yuriko_Koike&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Environmentalism")]

Having learned an environmental way of life from her own experience of wartime austerities in Egypt,[[10]](#cite_note-josei-10) Koike addresses environmental issues. She expressed the idea of introducing a [carbon tax](/wiki/Carbon_tax "Carbon tax") in 2005 so that Japan might achieve the goals of the [Kyoto Protocol](/wiki/Kyoto_Protocol "Kyoto Protocol").[[49]](#cite_note-50) The next year, she inaugurated the "[Mottainai](/wiki/Mottainai "Mottainai") Furoshiki" campaign, which urges shoppers to use [furoshiki](/wiki/Furoshiki "Furoshiki") in place of [plastic shopping bags](/wiki/Plastic_shopping_bag "Plastic shopping bag").[[50]](#cite_note-51) She is against the use of [biofuels](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel") made from food crops.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

### Nationalism and associated controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yuriko_Koike&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Nationalism and associated controversy")]

As a nationalist, Koike was one of the five vice secretaries general of the Diet Members' Committee of [Nippon Kaigi](/wiki/Nippon_Kaigi "Nippon Kaigi"), the country's largest conservative [think tank](/wiki/Think_tank "Think tank") and the main [nationalist](/wiki/Japanese_nationalism "Japanese nationalism") lobby, once chaired by [Tarō Asō](/wiki/Tar%C5%8D_As%C5%8D "Tarō Asō"). She is also known to have powerful ties to other large conservative political groups.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As a Governor of Tokyo, she has been criticized for years by [Japanese liberals](/wiki/Liberalism_in_Japan "Liberalism in Japan") and [Koreans](/wiki/Koreans "Koreans") in both Japan and the Koreas for refusing to acknowledge the occurrence of the 1923 [Kantō Massacre](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_Massacre "Kantō Massacre"), which mainly targeted ethnic Koreans.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-:1-55) Beginning in 2017, Koike broke decades of precedent by previous mayors by refusing to offer condolences to the descendants of survivors at an annual ceremony. She has since justified this by saying that whether a massacre occurred is a matter of historical debate.[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[54]](#cite_note-:1-55)

Koike's possible affiliation with a [far-right](/wiki/Far-right "Far-right") group was questioned in 2016, when a reporter asked about her speaking at a conference hosted by [Japan Women's Group Gentle Breeze](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Women%27s_Group_Gentle_Breeze&action=edit&redlink=1 "Japan Women's Group Gentle Breeze (page does not exist)") (日本女性の会そよ風; or "そよ風"), a women's non-profit with purported ties to [anti-Korean](/wiki/Anti-Korean_sentiment_in_Japan "Anti-Korean sentiment in Japan") hate group [Zaitokukai](/wiki/Zaitokukai "Zaitokukai"). In response, Koike stated that she wasn't aware of the non-profit's ties to Zaitokukai, and that she is invited to speak at many events.[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[54]](#cite_note-:1-55)

### Position on Article 9 of constitution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yuriko_Koike&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Position on Article 9 of constitution")]

Her foreign and security policies are often regarded as hawkish.[[22]](#cite_note-japantimes-22)[[57]](#cite_note-akahata-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59) She suggested that the prime minister revise the interpretation of [Article 9](/wiki/Article_9_of_the_Japanese_Constitution "Article 9 of the Japanese Constitution") of the [Constitution of Japan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan") to enable the government to exercise the right to collective self-defense.[[57]](#cite_note-akahata-58)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

She has supported the United States and the [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror") and opposes the Japanese government's tradition of [UN](/wiki/UN "UN")-centered foreign policy.[[60]](#cite_note-61) However, she has sent mixed messages to the United States in terms of destabilizing the Middle East with democratization efforts. On the other hand, showing parts of the world how powerful the United States is as an ally is a priority. During the 2008 LDP leadership election, she pledged to make [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") return [the four disputed islands](/wiki/Kuril_Islands_dispute "Kuril Islands dispute") to Japan if she was elected as prime minister. Back in 2010, she helped strengthen ties between Libya's Muammar Gaddafi and Japan. This led to the creation of the Japan-Libya Friendship Association.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

### Other positions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yuriko_Koike&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other positions")]

Koike has also actively promoted [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_popular_culture "Japanese popular culture") [pop culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture"), appearing in [cosplay](/wiki/Cosplay "Cosplay") as Sally from *[Sally the Witch](/wiki/Sally_the_Witch "Sally the Witch")* in 2015, and stating during her 2016 Tokyo gubernatorial campaign that she wanted to turn all of Tokyo into an "anime land".[[62]](#cite_note-63)

Koike initiated "Jisa Biz" (時差biz) in July 2017 to promote [remote work](/wiki/Remote_work "Remote work") and staggered work times to reduce [traffic congestion](/wiki/Traffic_congestion "Traffic congestion") during the morning [rush hour](/wiki/Rush_hour "Rush hour") in Tokyo.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

In 2017, Koike launched and led a new national political party. It was called [Kibō no Tō](/wiki/Kib%C5%8D_no_T%C5%8D "Kibō no Tō"), which means "[Party of Hope](/wiki/Party_of_Hope_(Japan) "Party of Hope (Japan)")". Although still Governor of Tokyo, she was the primary leader of this party. It was assumed that this party could have been the main opposition to the LDP. On 22 October 2017, the Party of Hope did not perform as well at the polls as expected. Koike's overarching policies were similar to those of Prime Minister Shinzo Abe. The policy to set them apart was their differing opinions on nuclear energy. Koike was opposed to it as an advocate of the environment. Koike did not join any successor party to the Party of Hope at its April 2018 dissolution.

In 2023, she ranked 62nd in Forbes list of "[World's 100 most powerful women](/wiki/Forbes_list_of_the_World%27s_100_Most_Powerful_Women "Forbes list of the World's 100 Most Powerful Women")".[[64]](#cite_note-65)
